blurb: A low woody-based evergreen cushion that pumps out hot-pink, snapdragon-like flowers from late spring to frost; a xeric, bee-and-hummingbird magnet that's borderline-hardy here.
sun: Full sun; afternoon shade in the hottest spots
water: Low; xeric once established — needs sharp drainage, not for wet ground
spread: Low woody-based mound spreading into a dense cushion; may self-sow
seasons: Nonstop pink flowers late spring to hard frost; small gray-green leaves evergreen in mild winters but killed back — or out — by hard Front-Range cold
wildlife: Bees, butterflies and hummingbirds work the tubular flowers all season
toxic: No documented toxicity to people or pets; also not a food plant
habitat: Rocky limestone slopes and dry scrub

care.hardiness: This is the honest catch: pink skullcap is rated only to about USDA zone 7, and Boulder sits at 5b–6a, so it is genuinely borderline here. In a hot, sharply-drained, sheltered microclimate (a south wall, a rock garden, a raised gravel bed) it can overwinter and behave as a short-lived woody perennial, but in an average winter it often dies back hard or dies out entirely; many Front-Range gardeners simply treat it as a warm-season annual or overwinter a potted plant. Site it in the warmest, driest spot you have and don't count on it as a permanent hedge or edging.[1][2]
care.planting: Set plants out after the mid-May last-frost date once the soil has warmed, and give them the sharpest drainage you can — a gravelly, lean bed or a slope, never a low wet spot. It grows beautifully in a container of fast-draining cactus/gritty mix, which is often the smartest way to keep it here: the pot drains hard in summer and can be moved into an unheated garage or cold frame to escape the worst winter cold, since potted roots are far less cold-hardy than the top and would freeze solid in an exposed pot.[2][3]
care.sun: Full sun gives the densest cushion and the heaviest, longest bloom. In the hottest, most reflective sites a little afternoon shade is fine and won't hurt flowering, but too much shade thins the plant and cuts the bloom.[1][2]
care.soil: Sharp drainage is the whole game. It wants sandy, gravelly or rocky soil and is intolerant of wet feet — soggy ground, especially in winter, rots the crown and is the usual way it's lost here. It takes a wide pH range from neutral into strongly alkaline (over 8.0), so Boulder's alkaline clay is fine chemically as long as you open the drainage with grit.[1][2]
care.water: Low. Water regularly through the first season to establish the roots, then back off sharply — once settled it is genuinely drought- and dry-soil tolerant and needs little supplemental water even in the heat of summer. Overwatering is more likely to kill it than drought. An occasional deep winter watering in a snowless dry spell helps an establishing plant survive.[1][2][3]
care.spacing: Each plant mounds to roughly 12–18 in across, so space them about 12–15 in apart to knit into a continuous cushion for edging or a small-scale groundcover.[1][2]
care.propagation: Easiest from softwood or semi-ripe stem cuttings taken in summer, which root readily and are the reliable way to keep a plant going through winter as an insurance backup. It can also self-seed modestly where it's happy, and those volunteers can be moved while small.[1][2]
care.feeding: Undemanding — it performs well in lean, rocky soil and needs little or no fertilizer. Skip rich feeding, which pushes soft, floppy growth that flowers less and is even less winter-hardy; a spot of gritty topdressing is plenty.[1][2]
care.pruning: Being woody-based, it takes a light shear rather than a hard cutback. Wait until spring, and only once you can see where new growth is breaking, then trim the previous year's stems back to just above that live growth to reshape the mound and encourage dense flowering; you can also remove obvious deadwood in fall. The critical rule for this and other woody subshrubs (like lavender): never cut down into bare, leafless old wood, which usually will not resprout and can kill the branch or the plant. Light shearing after the first big flush can also tidy it and prompt rebloom.[1][2]
care.maintenance: Very low-care once sited well. It blooms continuously without deadheading, so upkeep is mostly the spring shear above plus pulling the occasional self-sown seedling. In marginal winters, a loose gravel or evergreen-bough mulch over the crown improves its odds; avoid a heavy, moisture-holding organic mulch that keeps the crown wet.[1][2]
care.bloom: The show is long and generous: tiny, tubular, two-lipped, snapdragon-like flowers to about ¾ in, pink to rose, carried nonstop from late spring (roughly May) through fall until hard frost over the small, oval, thyme-like gray-green leaves.[1][2]
care.troubles: Generally trouble-free. The real danger here is cultural — crown and root rot from wet or poorly-drained soil, especially over winter. Otherwise watch for occasional powdery mildew or leaf spot in humid, crowded conditions and aphids on soft new growth; none are usually serious on a lean, airy, well-drained plant.[1][2]

edible.summary: Grown purely as an ornamental pollinator plant — no part is used as food.[1]
edible.caution: Pink skullcap has no documented culinary use and is not a food plant. Don't confuse it with the medicinal skullcaps of herbal traditions (Scutellaria lateriflora, S. baicalensis) — those are different species and this one is not a substitute for them. It is not listed as notably poisonous to people or pets, but an absence of a toxicity record is not the same as proof of safety, so enjoy it in the garden and don't eat it.[1][2]
